title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays 2023 data comparing the disease burden from non-communicable diseases against GDP per capita for various countries, with GDP per capita expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The vertical axis measures DALYs per 100,000 individuals, ranging from 0 to 50,000, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita on a logarithmic scale from $2,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, and circle sizes correspond to population. Nauru sits at the highest point on the chart, exceeding 50,000 DALYs per 100,000. The Solomon Islands, Marshall Islands, and Haiti form a cluster in the upper-left quadrant, with values between 35,000 and 45,000 DALYs and GDP per capita below $5,000. In the mid-range of the vertical axis, countries like Myanmar, Egypt, and Fiji appear between 30,000 and 35,000 DALYs. A dense grouping of African nations, including Burundi, DR Congo, Niger, and Mozambique, clusters on the far left with GDP per capita under $2,000 and DALY rates generally between 20,000 and 30,000. Large Asian populations such as India and China appear as large circles in the middle of the chart, with DALY rates near 20,000 to 25,000 and GDP per capita between $5,000 and $20,000. Conversely, high-income nations like the United States, Germany, Japan, and Singapore are positioned on the right side with GDP per capita above $40,000, showing DALY rates mostly between 10,000 and 25,000. Singapore sits near the bottom right with one of the lowest DALY rates, below 15,000.
